name: Uruguay
tagline: From the Charrua of the Banda Oriental and a contested frontier between the Spanish and Portuguese empires to Artigas and the Thirty-Three Orientals, the 1828 buffer state between Argentina and Brazil, the reforms of Batlle y Ordonez, the dictatorship of 1973-1985, and a stable, progressive democracy.
summary: Uruguay's history begins with the Charrua and other Indigenous peoples of the grasslands north of the Rio de la Plata, in a land the Spanish called the Banda Oriental, the eastern bank of the river. For two centuries it was a contested borderland between the Spanish and Portuguese empires, marked by the smuggling port of Colonia del Sacramento and the Spanish founding of Montevideo in 1724-1726. Independence came hard: the federalist hero Jose Gervasio Artigas, Portuguese and Brazilian occupation, the landing of the Thirty-Three Orientals and the declaration of 25 August 1825, and the British-brokered settlement of 1828 that created Uruguay as a buffer state between its giant neighbours. After a turbulent nineteenth century of civil war between Blancos and Colorados, the reforms of Jose Batlle y Ordonez made Uruguay an early welfare-state democracy, before a military dictatorship from 1973 to 1985 gave way to the stable, progressive republic of today.

founding.label: Independence settled by the 1828 Treaty of Montevideo
founding.detail: Modern Uruguay was born of the 1825-1828 war between the United Provinces of the Rio de la Plata (Argentina) and the Empire of Brazil for control of the Banda Oriental, which Brazil had annexed as the Cisplatine Province. The struggle was reignited on 25 August 1825, when the Thirty-Three Orientals led by Juan Antonio Lavalleja, having landed on the coast that April, proclaimed the province's separation from Brazil and union with the United Provinces. Neither neighbour could prevail, and under British mediation the Preliminary Peace Convention, signed in 1828 and known as the Treaty of Montevideo, ended the war by establishing the disputed territory as an independent buffer state, the Oriental Republic of Uruguay. The first national constitution was sworn in 1830.

quickFacts.label: Founding peoples
quickFacts.value: Charrua and other Indigenous peoples of the Banda Oriental

quickFacts.label: Montevideo founded
quickFacts.value: Spanish settlement begun 1724, formally founded 1726 by Bruno Mauricio de Zabala

quickFacts.label: Statehood
quickFacts.value: Created as a buffer state by the 1828 Treaty of Montevideo

quickFacts.label: Welfare-state reforms
quickFacts.value: Jose Batlle y Ordonez, president 1903-1907 and 1911-1915

quickFacts.label: Military dictatorship
quickFacts.value: June 1973 to March 1985; democracy restored under Sanguinetti

eras.title: The Charrua and the Banda Oriental
eras.standfirst: Long before European sails appeared, the grasslands north of the Rio de la Plata were home to the Charrua and other Indigenous peoples; the Spanish who explored the estuary from 1516 found little to detain them, and for nearly two centuries the Banda Oriental remained an empty, contested frontier.
body: The land that became Uruguay was inhabited for thousands of years by Indigenous hunting and gathering peoples, of whom the best known are the Charrua, who occupied the grasslands north of the Rio de la Plata in a territory somewhat larger than the modern country. Skilled bowmen who also used the bolas and the sling, the Charrua were a mobile people who, after the horse and wild cattle spread across the plains, lived in good part by hunting feral herds. Fierce in war, they resisted outsiders with a tenacity that became part of national legend, and they gave Uruguayans an enduring word for themselves.

European contact began in 1516, when the Spanish navigator Juan Diaz de Solis entered the great estuary of the Rio de la Plata and was killed, by tradition at the hands of Indigenous people, near its eastern shore. The Spanish found neither the gold nor the dense settled populations that drew them to Mexico and Peru, and so for nearly two hundred years the Banda Oriental, the eastern bank, remained a neglected margin of empire. Its grasslands filled instead with the descendants of cattle and horses turned loose by early expeditions, wealth on the hoof that drew gauchos, hide-hunters and smugglers.

Because the region was a frontier between two empires rather than a prize in itself, it was long defined by what surrounded it. To the west lay the Spanish settlements of the Rio de la Plata; to the north and east lay Portuguese Brazil, pressing steadily southward. The absence of mineral wealth and settled towns meant that, when the rival crowns finally turned their attention to the Banda Oriental in the later seventeenth century, they did so chiefly to deny it to one another, setting the stage for a long colonial contest fought over a thinly peopled land.

events.title: Indigenous peoples of the grasslands
events.summary: The Charrua and other hunting and gathering peoples occupy the plains north of the Rio de la Plata over many millennia, well before any European arrival.

events.title: Solis reaches the Rio de la Plata
events.summary: The Spanish navigator Juan Diaz de Solis explores the estuary and is killed near its eastern shore; finding no gold, Spain largely ignores the Banda Oriental for nearly two centuries.

events.title: Cattle turn the plains to wealth
events.summary: Cattle and horses introduced by early expeditions multiply across the grasslands into vast feral herds, drawing gauchos, hide-hunters and contrabandists to the frontier.

pullquote.text: The eastern bank held neither silver nor cities, only grass, wild cattle, and the Charrua; for two centuries the empires let it lie.
pullquote.attribution: On the Banda Oriental before colonisation

eras.title: The Contested Frontier: Colonia and Montevideo
eras.standfirst: Portugal's founding of Colonia del Sacramento in 1680 opened a century of rivalry over the Banda Oriental; to block the Portuguese, Spain expelled their garrison and founded Montevideo in 1724-1726, creating the fortified port around which the nation would grow.
body: In 1680 Portuguese soldiers founded Colonia del Sacramento on the Rio de la Plata, directly across the water from Spanish Buenos Aires. Its position made it both a strategic foothold and a smuggling entrepot, through which European goods could be slipped past Spain's trade restrictions into the silver economy of the Plata. For more than a century Colonia changed hands repeatedly between the two crowns, besieged, captured and returned by treaty again and again, until its founders finally lost it for good. Its well-preserved old town, where Portuguese, Spanish and post-colonial styles meet, was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage site in 1995.

Alarmed by the Portuguese presence, Spain moved to secure the bay a short way up the coast. In late 1723 the Portuguese had begun to fortify the natural harbour of Montevideo; in early 1724 a Spanish force under Bruno Mauricio de Zabala, governor of Buenos Aires, expelled them and began a settlement of their own. The town of San Felipe y Santiago de Montevideo was formally established by 1726, its fine harbour and ring of walls making it the chief Spanish stronghold on the eastern bank and a rival to Buenos Aires for the river trade.

Through the eighteenth century the Banda Oriental slowly filled in. Cattle ranches, the estancias, spread across the interior; Montevideo grew into a fortified port and, by the 1770s, the seat of a Spanish naval station. When the Viceroyalty of the Rio de la Plata was created in 1776, the region was drawn firmly into the Spanish administrative orbit centred on Buenos Aires, even as resentment of that centralising capital began to take root among the cattlemen and townspeople of the eastern shore.

events.title: Portugal founds Colonia del Sacramento
events.summary: Portuguese soldiers establish Colonia opposite Buenos Aires; it becomes a strategic outpost and smuggling centre, changing hands between the crowns for over a century.

events.title: Spain expels the Portuguese at Montevideo
events.summary: A Spanish force under Bruno Mauricio de Zabala drives out a Portuguese garrison that had begun fortifying the bay of Montevideo, opening the way for a Spanish town.

events.title: Founding of Montevideo
events.summary: Zabala formally establishes Montevideo as a fortified Spanish port to counter Portuguese expansion; it grows into the chief stronghold of the Banda Oriental.

events.title: Viceroyalty of the Rio de la Plata
events.summary: Spain creates the new viceroyalty centred on Buenos Aires, drawing the Banda Oriental into its administration and stoking eastern resentment of the centralising capital.

events.title: Colonia listed by UNESCO
events.summary: The historic quarter of Colonia del Sacramento, blending Portuguese, Spanish and post-colonial architecture, is inscribed as a World Heritage site.

figures.name: Bruno Mauricio de Zabala
figures.blurb: Governor of Buenos Aires who expelled the Portuguese from the bay of Montevideo and founded the fortified Spanish town there in the 1720s to block Portuguese expansion into the Banda Oriental.

pullquote.text: Colonia was a door for smugglers, Montevideo the lock Spain built against it; the nation grew up between them.
pullquote.attribution: On the colonial frontier of the Rio de la Plata

eras.title: Artigas, the Thirty-Three, and Independence
eras.standfirst: The revolutions of the Rio de la Plata raised the gaucho leader Jose Gervasio Artigas and his federal vision; defeated by Portuguese invasion, his cause was taken up by the Thirty-Three Orientals, whose 1825 rising and the British-brokered settlement of 1828 finally made Uruguay a nation.
body: When the wars of independence broke over the Rio de la Plata after 1810, the Banda Oriental found its champion in Jose Gervasio Artigas, a former gaucho and frontier officer who became the foremost advocate of federalism against the centralising ambitions of Buenos Aires. Leading the Orientals in revolt against Spain, he built a federal league of provinces and, for a time, held sway over a vast region of what is now Uruguay and northern Argentina. His insistence on autonomy set him against Buenos Aires, and his radical land reforms alarmed the propertied classes, weakening his position even as he fought.

Artigas's undoing came from the north. Between 1816 and 1820 Portuguese forces from Brazil invaded the Banda Oriental, and despite three years of resistance Artigas was finally routed and driven into exile in Paraguay, where he lived out his life. The province was annexed by the Portuguese, and after Brazil's own independence it was absorbed into the new empire as the Cisplatine Province. Yet Artigas's vision of a free eastern republic survived him, and he is honoured as the father of Uruguayan independence.

In April 1825 a band of patriots known as the Thirty-Three Orientals, led by Juan Antonio Lavalleja, crossed from Argentine soil and landed on the Uruguayan coast to renew the struggle against Brazilian rule. On 25 August 1825 they proclaimed the province's independence from Brazil and its union with the United Provinces of the Rio de la Plata, igniting a war between Argentina and Brazil. Neither could win outright, and Britain, anxious to reopen the blockaded river to trade, mediated a settlement. The Preliminary Peace Convention of 1828, the Treaty of Montevideo, ended the war by making the disputed land an independent buffer state, and the first constitution of the Oriental Republic of Uruguay was sworn in 1830.

events.title: Artigas and the Federal League
events.summary: Jose Gervasio Artigas leads the Orientals against Spain and builds a federal league of provinces, championing autonomy against Buenos Aires across the Banda Oriental and beyond.

events.title: Portuguese invasion and Artigas in exile
events.summary: Portuguese forces from Brazil invade the Banda Oriental; after three years of resistance Artigas is defeated in 1820 and goes into permanent exile in Paraguay.

events.title: The Thirty-Three Orientals declare independence
events.summary: Juan Antonio Lavalleja and the Thirty-Three Orientals, having landed that April, proclaim separation from Brazil and union with the United Provinces, reigniting the war for the province.

events.title: Treaty of Montevideo creates Uruguay
events.summary: Under British mediation, the Preliminary Peace Convention ends the Argentine-Brazilian war by establishing the contested territory as the independent buffer state of Uruguay.

events.title: First constitution sworn
events.summary: The Oriental Republic of Uruguay adopts its first national constitution, formally organising the new state.

figures.name: Jose Gervasio Artigas
figures.blurb: Gaucho turned revolutionary who led the Orientals against Spain and championed federalism against Buenos Aires. Defeated by Portuguese invasion and exiled to Paraguay in 1820, he is revered as the founding hero of the nation he did not live to see.

figures.name: Juan Antonio Lavalleja
figures.blurb: Revolutionary general who led the Thirty-Three Orientals in their 1825 landing and proclaimed independence from Brazil, beginning the war that ended with Uruguayan statehood in 1828.

pullquote.text: Artigas gave the Orientals a flag and a cause; the Thirty-Three gave them a nation.
pullquote.attribution: On the long road to Uruguayan independence

eras.title: Blancos, Colorados, and the Guerra Grande
eras.standfirst: The young republic was torn for decades by the rivalry of its two great parties, the Blancos and the Colorados; the Guerra Grande of 1839-1851 left Montevideo besieged for nine years, while the state extinguished the Charrua and slowly, after midcentury, settled into a prosperous cattle economy.
body: Independence did not bring peace. From its earliest years Uruguay was divided between two factions that hardened into the parties dominating its politics ever after: the Colorados, identified with Montevideo, liberalism and the colour red, and the Blancos, or Nationalists, rooted in the rural interior and conservatism and marked by white. Their rivalry, entangled with the civil wars of Argentina, exploded into the Guerra Grande of 1839 to 1851, the longest and hardest war in the country's history, in which the Blanco Manuel Oribe besieged Montevideo for nine years while the Colorado Fructuoso Rivera held the city.

The conflict drew in Argentina's dictator Juan Manuel de Rosas, who backed the Blancos, and the intervention of Britain, France and a legion of Italian volunteers under Giuseppe Garibaldi, who fought alongside the defenders of Montevideo. The war finally ended in 1851 without a clear victor, leaving the country exhausted but its independence intact. In the same era the new state turned violently on the Indigenous Charrua: in 1831 government forces ambushed and massacred them at Salsipuedes, a campaign remembered as the moment that destroyed the Charrua as a people.

After midcentury Uruguay began to stabilise and prosper. The vast herds of the interior fed a booming export trade in hides, salted beef and, increasingly, wool, while British investment built railways and modernised Montevideo's port. Waves of European immigrants, above all Italians and Spaniards, swelled the cities and the countryside, remaking the population. Politics remained turbulent and often militarised, but beneath the upheavals the foundations of a modern, outward-looking economy were being laid, setting the scene for the reforms that would soon transform the republic.

events.title: Massacre of the Charrua at Salsipuedes
events.summary: Government forces ambush and massacre the Charrua at Salsipuedes, a campaign that destroyed them as a distinct people and is remembered as a foundational injustice.

events.title: The Guerra Grande
events.summary: The rivalry of Colorados and Blancos erupts into the Guerra Grande; the Blanco Oribe besieges Montevideo for nine years while the Colorados, aided by foreign legions, hold the city.

events.title: End of the long war
events.summary: The Guerra Grande ends without outright victory, leaving Uruguay exhausted but independent and its two-party rivalry entrenched.

events.title: Cattle economy and immigration
events.summary: Booming exports of hides, beef and wool, British-built railways, and waves of Italian and Spanish immigrants remake Montevideo and the countryside.

pullquote.text: For nine years the Blancos camped outside the walls and the Colorados held the port; Montevideo lived its longest siege.

eras.title: Batlle y Ordonez and the Model Country
eras.standfirst: Under Jose Batlle y Ordonez, twice president in the new century, Uruguay built one of the world's earliest welfare states, with the vote, the eight-hour day, free education and pensions, earning a reputation as the stable, prosperous Switzerland of South America.
body: The transformation of Uruguay is bound up with one man, Jose Batlle y Ordonez of the Colorado Party, president from 1903 to 1907 and again from 1911 to 1915. A reformer of extraordinary range, he set out to remake the republic into a model of democracy and social justice. His governments introduced sweeping labour laws including the eight-hour day, nationalised key industries and public works, expanded free secular education, established pensions and welfare protections, abolished the death penalty, and championed the separation of church and state. So distinctive was his programme that it gave its name to a creed, Batllismo.

Batlle is generally credited with turning Uruguay into a stable democratic welfare state, the first of its kind in the Western Hemisphere. The prosperity of the cattle and wool economy underwrote his reforms, and for decades the country enjoyed a level of social peace, literacy and material comfort rare in the region, winning it the admiring epithet of the Switzerland of South America. Power was managed through bargaining between the Colorados and Blancos, and for a time Batlle even persuaded the country to replace the single presidency with a collegial executive, the colegiado, to guard against one-man rule.

The model country was not without strain. Batllismo depended on the export earnings of beef and wool, and when those markets faltered the costs of the welfare state grew harder to bear. A brief authoritarian interlude under President Gabriel Terra in the 1930s suspended the constitution before democracy was restored, and the collegial experiment was tried, abandoned, and tried again. Yet through the first half of the twentieth century Uruguay remained, by the standards of Latin America, a remarkably democratic, literate and egalitarian society, living on the inheritance Batlle had built.

events.title: Batlle y Ordonez becomes president
events.summary: Jose Batlle y Ordonez of the Colorado Party wins the presidency, beginning the reform era that would make Uruguay an early welfare-state democracy.

events.title: The great reforms
events.summary: In his second term Batlle enacts the eight-hour day, free education, pensions, nationalisations, abolition of the death penalty and church-state separation, founding the model welfare state.

events.title: A new welfare constitution
events.summary: A reformed constitution entrenches the secular, democratic and social character of the Batllista republic, including a plural executive to check one-man power.

events.title: Terra's authoritarian interlude
events.summary: Amid the Great Depression President Gabriel Terra suspends the constitution in a self-coup; constitutional democracy is restored later in the decade.

figures.name: Jose Batlle y Ordonez
figures.blurb: Twice president of Uruguay and its most influential statesman, he built one of the world's first welfare states, with labour laws, free education, pensions and church-state separation, earning his country the name Switzerland of South America.

pullquote.text: He gave Uruguay the eight-hour day, free schools, pensions and a secular state, and a name: the Switzerland of South America.

eras.title: Dictatorship, Recovery, and Modern Uruguay
eras.standfirst: Economic decline and the Tupamaro insurgency drew the army into power; the dictatorship of 1973 to 1985 jailed and tortured thousands before democracy returned, and modern Uruguay has become a byword for stability and progressive reform.
body: By the 1950s and 1960s the Batllista welfare state was straining as export earnings stagnated and inflation rose, eroding the prosperity that had paid for it. Out of the discontent emerged the Tupamaros, a leftist urban guerrilla movement founded around 1963 and led by Raul Sendic, who turned from Robin Hood gestures to bank raids, kidnappings and assassinations. The state responded with mounting repression, and as the crisis deepened the armed forces took an ever larger role in government, until on 27 June 1973 the president dissolved parliament with military backing and a civic-military dictatorship took hold.

The dictatorship that ruled from 1973 to 1985 must be remembered soberly. It crushed the Tupamaros, killing some and imprisoning thousands, and extended its repression far beyond them, banning parties, censoring the press, and detaining, torturing and exiling large numbers of citizens; Uruguay came to hold one of the highest rates of political imprisonment in the world. Among those jailed for over a decade was a former Tupamaro, Jose Mujica. Gradually the regime lost legitimacy, and after a failed attempt to entrench military rule by plebiscite in 1980, negotiations opened a path back to civilian government.

Elections in 1984 returned the Colorado Julio Maria Sanguinetti to the presidency, and democracy was restored on 1 March 1985, with political prisoners freed under an amnesty. In the decades since, Uruguay has earned a reputation as one of Latin America's most stable, least corrupt and most socially progressive democracies. The left-wing Frente Amplio coalition broke the old two-party duopoly in 2004, and under presidents Tabare Vazquez and Jose Mujica the country legalised abortion, same-sex marriage and, in 2013, became the first nation in the world to fully legalise and regulate cannabis. Mujica, the once-imprisoned guerrilla turned modest head of state, came to symbolise this modern Uruguay, whose history runs from a contested frontier to a quietly admired republic in 2026.

events.title: Rise of the Tupamaros
events.summary: The leftist urban guerrilla movement of the Tupamaros forms under Raul Sendic, turning to robberies, kidnappings and assassinations amid economic decline.

events.title: Military takes power
events.summary: With the Tupamaros crushed and the country in crisis, the president dissolves parliament with military backing, beginning a civic-military dictatorship.

events.title: Return to democracy
events.summary: After elections in 1984, Julio Maria Sanguinetti is inaugurated president, restoring civilian rule and freeing political prisoners under an amnesty.

events.title: Frente Amplio breaks the duopoly
events.summary: The left-wing Frente Amplio coalition wins the presidency for the first time, ending nearly two centuries of Colorado and Blanco dominance.

events.title: Cannabis and progressive reform
events.summary: Under President Jose Mujica, Uruguay legalises same-sex marriage and becomes the first country in the world to fully legalise and regulate cannabis.

figures.name: Jose Mujica
figures.blurb: Former Tupamaro guerrilla who spent some thirteen years in prison under the dictatorship, then served as president from 2010 to 2015. Famed for his austere lifestyle, he oversaw landmark progressive reforms and became a symbol of modern Uruguay.

pullquote.text: From one of the world's highest rates of political imprisonment to a quietly admired democracy: Uruguay's hardest passage.
pullquote.attribution: On the dictatorship and the return to democracy

figures.name: Jose Gervasio Artigas
figures.blurb: The gaucho leader and federalist hero who led the Orientals against Spain and is revered as the founding father of the nation, though he died in exile before its independence was secured.

figures.name: Jose Batlle y Ordonez
figures.blurb: Twice president and the architect of Uruguay's early welfare state, whose reforms made the country a model democracy known as the Switzerland of South America.

figures.name: Jose Mujica
figures.blurb: Former guerrilla and political prisoner who became Uruguay's most internationally admired leader, embodying its stable, progressive modern democracy.

sources.label: Uruguay: The struggle for national identity
Historic Quarter of the City of Colonia del Sacramento
A Guide to the United States' History of Recognition, Diplomatic, and Consular Relations: Uruguay
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian
